instagram_reel.text:
La mayoría de los agentes no fallan por inteligencia. Fallan porque pierden el hilo entre sesiones.

En este demo, `SESSION-STATE.md` aparece en medio del trabajo, la decisión clave queda escrita en memoria durable, y después termina en Obsidian sin cleanup manual.

Si querés ver más workflows reales así, suscribite a NODO.

cta.reason_for_now: because most multi-session agent work fails on memory drift before it fails on raw capability

hook_variants: El problema no es el modelo. Es que tu agente pierde contexto.
Mirá cómo `SESSION-STATE.md` aparece en plena sesión y evita que el agente se desordene.
Esto es memoria operativa real, no vibes.

tweets.text: A lot of AI agents do something impressive for 2 minutes, then lose the plot. The problem is not just reasoning. It is missing operational memory.
One pattern I like in OpenClaw is explicit session memory. Not 'the model probably remembers.' A real file in the repo. Visible. Auditable. Updateable.
The setup is simple: `MEMORY.md` stores durable facts, `SESSION-STATE.md` stores live working memory, and the agent writes to it before replying.
`SESSION-STATE.md` acts like a WAL, so the session keeps current objective, key context, pending actions, and verification state without re-deriving everything from scratch.
What matters is not just that it remembers. You can inspect what the system thinks is true right now, which makes debugging and handoffs much less fragile.
Then that session memory can be flushed into longer-term journaling, including an Obsidian workflow on the archive side. Less drift now, better context later.

If you're building with Claude Code, OpenClaw, or multi-agent workflows, this is one of the patterns worth stealing. Explicit memory beats vibes. More real operator workflows in NODO: https://maxtechera.com/newsletter

cta.reason_for_now: to steal the memory layer before your next agent run loses state again
